g.data-narration: Welcome to Two-Phase Locking Techniques - a fundamental concurrency control method ensuring database consistency.
g.text: for Concurrency Control in Database Management Systems

g.data-narration: When multiple transactions run simultaneously, problems like lost updates and inconsistent data can occur without proper control.
Two-Phase Locking ensures serializability by requiring all locks before releasing any - guaranteeing consistent database execution.
The growing phase acquires locks while the shrinking phase releases them - the order must never reverse, defining the lock point.
In Basic 2PL, Transaction T1 acquires locks on A and B during growth, then releases them during shrinking phase.
g.text: Xlock(A) → Read → Xlock(B) → Read → Write → Unlock(A) → Write → Unlock(B)

g.data-narration: Strict 2PL holds exclusive write locks until commit, preventing cascading aborts from uncommitted data being read by others.
g.text: ✓ Prevents cascading rollbacks | ✗ Read locks released normally

g.data-narration: Rigorous 2PL retains ALL locks until commit, completely eliminating cascading aborts - used by most commercial databases.
g.text: ALL locks (Read + Write) held until COMMIT/ABORT
Oracle, SQL Server, MySQL - simplifies recovery
✓ Complete prevention of cascading aborts | ✓ Simplest recovery | ✗ Reduced concurrency

g.data-narration: Compare the three 2PL variants: Basic allows early release causing cascades, Strict releases only write at commit, Rigorous holds all.
g.text: Comparison: Basic vs Strict vs Rigorous 2PL

g.data-narration: The lock compatibility matrix shows which locks can coexist: Shared locks allow multiple readers, but Exclusive blocks all others.
g.text: • X-Lock blocks ALL other locks (writer exclusion)

g.data-narration: Deadlock occurs when transactions circularly wait for locks held by each other, forming a cycle in the wait-for graph.
Wait-Die allows older transactions to wait while younger ones die. Wound-Wait lets older wound younger, saving them from restart.
g.text: Older TX waits for younger TX | Younger TX dies (aborts)
Older TX wounds (aborts) younger | Younger TX waits
Example: T1(older) wants lock held by T2(younger) → T1 waits (Wait-Die) OR T1 wounds T2 (Wound-Wait)

g.data-narration: Deadlock detection uses wait-for graphs to find cycles. When found, a victim transaction is selected and aborted to break it.
g.text: Victim selection criteria: Least cost, least progress, fewest locks, youngest timestamp

g.data-narration: Timestamp protocols offer an alternative to locks - using transaction timestamps to order operations, avoiding deadlocks entirely.
g.text: Each transaction gets timestamp at start | Operations execute only if valid timestamp
Modified TO allowing certain out-of-order writes as blind writes | Improves concurrency

g.data-narration: Thomas's Write Rule improves timestamp ordering by allowing stale writes to proceed as blind writes instead of rejecting them.
g.text: If TS(T) < W-timestamp(X): Write rejected in Basic TO
Thomas's: Allow as blind write, ignore old value
T1 writes X (TS=10) | T2 writes X (TS=20) late
Basic TO rejects T2 | Thomas's accepts (X = T2's value)
Result: Better concurrency while maintaining conflict serializability

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: 2PL ensures serializability through growing and shrinking phases, with Strict and Rigorous variants preventing cascades.
g.text: Commercial databases use Rigorous 2PL (or variations) for simplest recovery

g.data-narration: Exam tips: Remember growing precedes shrinking, draw wait-for graphs for deadlock, and memorize the compatibility matrix.
g.text: Master these concepts to excel in your university exams!
